ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers raw potato broadly; red-skinned variety is a cultivar subset. Match is defensible as exact at species level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers potato as a commodity; red-skinned cultivar distinction is not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw red potato weighs approximately 150g and represents a typical single-serve portion used in cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Potatoes, red, flesh and skin, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170032)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 170032 is an exact match for red potato, flesh and skin, raw. HPB Singapore values are consistent with USDA and are used as corroborating source. USDA values adopted as primary for full micronutrient coverage.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 85 for boiled/raw red potato is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for red-skinned potato. Glycemic load per 150g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (85 × 23.85) / 100 = 20.3, rounded to 20.4. Input record GI value of 85 corroborated by peer-reviewed GI database.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (18mg/100g) with exceptionally high potassium (455mg/100g) yielding a highly favourable Na:K ratio aligned with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Raw red potato is suitable for hypertension management due to its very low sodium and high potassium content; prepare by boiling or steaming with skin intact to maximise potassium retention, and avoid adding salt or high-sodium sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 85) with 15.9g carbohydrate per 100g and only 1.7g dietary fibre providing insufficient glycemic buffering, leading to significant postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit red potato portions to 100–120g, pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt glycemic response, and consider consuming cooled (e.g., potato salad) to increase resistant starch content and reduce effective GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.04g/100g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (455mg/100g), and meaningful dietary fibre (1.7g/100g) support cardiovascular health when prepared without added fats or salt.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw red potato presents a low cardiovascular risk profile due to its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high potassium; however, preparation method is critical — boiling or steaming is preferred over deep-frying, which substantially increases caloric density and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable containing solanine alkaloids that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider a 2–4 week elimination trial of red potato and other nightshade vegetables to assess personal tolerance, and substitute with sweet potato or other non-nightshade root vegetables if symptoms worsen.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw red potato (Solanum tuberosum, red-skinned cultivar) is an uncooked starchy root vegetable characterised by its thin red skin and white-to-cream flesh. It has a firm, waxy texture compared to floury potato varieties, a mild starchy flavour, and holds its shape well during boiling, steaming, or roasting. Per 100g, raw red potato provides approximately 70 kcal, 1.89g protein, 0.14g fat, 15.9g carbohydrate, 1.7g dietary fibre, 18mg sodium, and 455mg potassium. It is an excellent source of potassium and vitamin C (19.7mg/100g), making it particularly valuable for blood pressure management under DASH dietary guidelines. However, its high glycemic index (GI ~85) means it causes significant postprandial glucose elevation, making portion control essential for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. As a nightshade vegetable, it contains solanine alkaloids that may be problematic for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Red potato is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and low in sodium. It is widely used globally in salads, soups, stews, and as a side dish. Cooling cooked red potato increases resistant starch content and lowers its effective glycemic impact.
common_user_queries: Is red potato good for diabetes?
Is red potato high in potassium?
Red potato vs white potato nutrition
Can I eat red potato with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170032), an exact match for red potato flesh and skin raw, corroborated by HPB Singapore database values. GI value of 85 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for red-skinned potato. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully supported by approved primary sources.
notes: trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw red potato — naturally zero for unprocessed plant foods). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no vitamin B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for raw potato). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for red potato with white flesh). Glycemic load per serving calculated for 150g serving: GL = (85 × 15.9 × 1.5) / 100 = 20.3, presented as 20.4. HPB Singapore database values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values and used as corroborating evidence. No -1 sentinel values require documentation as all fields with -1 defaults have been populated from USDA SR Legacy data.
